revision.text:
{{Infobox writer <!-- for more information see [[:Template:Infobox writer/doc]] --> | name = Senge Motomaro | image = Senge Motomaro.jpg | caption = Senge Motomaro | birth_date = {{birth date|1888|6|8|df=y}} | birth_place = [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]] | death_date = {{death date and age|1948|3|14|1888|6|8|df=y}} | death_place = | occupation = Writer | genre = poetry | movement = | notableworks = | influences = | influenced = }} {{family name hatnote|Senge|lang=Japanese}} {{nihongo|'''Senge Motomaro'''|千家 元麿||extra=8 June 1888 – 14 March 1948}} was a [[Japanese people|Japanese]] [[Japanese poetry|poet]] active during the [[Taishō period|Taishō]] and [[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] periods of [[Japan]].
==Biography== Motomaro Senge was born in [[Tokyo]] as the younger son of the [[Shinto]] high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]] in [[Shimane Prefecture]], who was also a member of the [[Japanese House of Peers|House of Peers]]. He was a member of the ''[[Shirakaba]]'' ("White Birch") literary circle, and published many of his poems in their [[literary magazine]]. Save for xenophobic poems written during World War II, his poetry reflects the philosophy of [[humanism]] with an optimistic perspective on the world. He was a prolific author, publishing as many as 30-40 works per month. His poems tend toward minimalism and describe everyday events and scenes, without resort to excessive sentimentality.
His anthology, ''Jibun wa mita'' (“I Saw”, 1918), contains the poem ''Kuruma no oto'' (“Noise of the Carts”), which often appears in Japanese collections of Taisho period poetry. His longer work, ''Mukashi no ie'' (“House of Long Ago”, 1929), is [[autobiography|autobiographical]], describing his aristocratic background.<ref>''Japan: An Illustrated Encyclopedia'', Kodansha, vol. 2, p. 1345,</ref>
==See also== *[[Japanese literature]] *[[List of Japanese authors]]
<references/>
==External links== *[http://www.aozora.gr.jp/index_pages/person617.html e-texts of works] at [[Aozora Bunko]]
{{Authority control}}

{{Infobox writer <!-- for more information see [[:Template:Infobox writer/doc]] --> | name = Senge Motomaro | image = Senge Motomaro.jpg | caption = Senge Motomaro | birth_date = {{birth date|1888|6|8|df=y}} | birth_place = [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]] | death_date = {{death date and age|1948|3|14|1888|6|8|df=y}} | death_place = | occupation = Writer | genre = poetry | movement = | notableworks = | influences = | influenced = }} {{family name hatnote|Senge|lang=Japanese}} {{nihongo|'''Senge Motomaro'''|千家 元麿||extra=8 June 1888 – 14 March 1948}} was a [[Japanese people|Japanese]] [[Japanese poetry|poet]] active during the [[Taishō period|Taishō]] and [[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] periods of [[Japan]].
==Biography== Motomaro Senge was born in [[Tokyo]] as the younger son of the [[Shinto]] high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]] in [[Shimane Prefecture]], who was also a member of the [[Japanese House of Peers|House of Peers]]. He was a member of the ''[[Shirakaba]]'' ("White Birch") literary circle, and published many of his poems in their [[literary magazine]]. Save for xenophobic poems written during World War II, his poetry reflects the philosophy of [[humanism]] with an optimistic perspective on the world. He was a prolific author, publishing as many as 30-40 works per month. His poems tend toward minimalism and describe everyday events and scenes, without resort to excessive sentimentality.
His anthology, ''Jibun wa mita'' (“I Saw”, 1918), contains the poem ''Kuruma no oto'' (“Noise of the Carts”), which often appears in Japanese collections of Taisho period poetry. His longer work, ''Mukashi no ie'' (“House of Long Ago”, 1929), is [[autobiography|autobiographical]], describing his aristocratic background.<ref>''Japan: An Illustrated Encyclopedia'', Kodansha, vol. 2, p. 1345,</ref>

{{Infobox writer <!-- for more information see [[:Template:Infobox writer/doc]] --> | name = Senge Motomaro | image = Senge Motomaro.jpg | caption = Senge Motomaro | birth_date = {{birth date|1888|6|8|df=y}} | birth_place = [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]] | death_date = {{death date and age|1948|3|14|1888|6|8|df=y}} | death_place = | occupation = Writer | genre = poetry | movement = | notableworks = | influences = | influenced = }} {{family name hatnote|Senge|lang=Japanese}} {{nihongo|'''Senge Motomaro'''|千家 元麿||extra=8 June 1888 – 14 March 1948}} was a [[Japanese people|Japanese]] [[Japanese poetry|poet]] active during the [[Taishō period|Taishō]] and [[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] periods of [[Japan]].
==Biography== Motomaro Senge was born in [[Tokyo]] as the younger son of the [[Shinto]] high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]] in [[Shimane Prefecture]], who was also a member of the [[Japanese House of Peers|House of Peers]]. He was a member of the ''[[Shirakaba]]'' ("White Birch") literary circle, and published many of his poems in their [[literary magazine]]. Save for xenophobic poems written during World War II, his poetry reflects the philosophy of [[humanism]] with an optimistic perspective on the world. He was a prolific author, publishing as many as 30-40 works per month. His poems tend toward minimalism and describe everyday events and scenes, without resort to excessive sentimentality.
His anthology, ''Jibun wa mita'' (“I Saw”, 1918), contains the poem ''Kuruma no oto'' (“Noise of the Carts”), which often appears in Japanese collections of Taisho period poetry. His longer work, ''Mukashi no ie'' (“House of Long Ago”, 1929), is [[autobiography|autobiographical]], describing his aristocratic background.<ref>''Japan: An Illustrated Encyclopedia'', Kodansha, vol. 2, p. 1345,</ref>
==See also== *[[Japanese literature]] *[[List of Japanese authors]]
<references/>
==External links== *[http://www.aozora.gr.jp/index_pages/person617.html e-texts of works] at [[Aozora Bunko]]
{{Authority control}}

==Biography== Motomaro Senge was born in [[Tokyo]] as the younger son of the [[Shinto]] high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]] in [[Shimane Prefecture]], who was also a member of the [[Japanese House of Peers|House of Peers]]. He was a member of the ''[[Shirakaba]]'' ("White Birch") literary circle, and published many of his poems in their [[literary magazine]]. Save for xenophobic poems written during World War II, his poetry reflects the philosophy of [[humanism]] with an optimistic perspective on the world. He was a prolific author, publishing as many as 30-40 works per month. His poems tend toward minimalism and describe everyday events and scenes, without resort to excessive sentimentality.
His anthology, ''Jibun wa mita'' (“I Saw”, 1918), contains the poem ''Kuruma no oto'' (“Noise of the Carts”), which often appears in Japanese collections of Taisho period poetry. His longer work, ''Mukashi no ie'' (“House of Long Ago”, 1929), is [[autobiography|autobiographical]], describing his aristocratic background.<ref>''Japan: An Illustrated Encyclopedia'', Kodansha, vol. 2, p. 1345,</ref>
==See also== *[[Japanese literature]] *[[List of Japanese authors]]
<references/>

{{Infobox writer <!-- for more information see [[:Template:Infobox writer/doc]] --> | name = Senge Motomaro | image = Senge Motomaro.jpg | caption = Senge Motomaro | birth_date = {{birth date|1888|6|8|df=y}} | birth_place = [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]] | death_date = {{death date and age|1948|3|14|1888|6|8|df=y}} | death_place = | occupation = Writer | genre = poetry | movement = | notableworks = | influences = | influenced = }} {{family name hatnote|Senge|lang=Japanese}} {{nihongo|'''Senge Motomaro'''|千家 元麿||extra=8 June 1888 – 14 March 1948}} was a [[Japanese people|Japanese]] [[Japanese poetry|poet]] active during the [[Taishō period|Taishō]] and [[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] periods of [[Japan]].
==Biography== Motomaro Senge was born in [[Tokyo]] as the younger son of the [[Shinto]] high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]] in [[Shimane Prefecture]], who was also a member of the [[Japanese House of Peers|House of Peers]]. He was a member of the ''[[Shirakaba]]'' ("White Birch") literary circle, and published many of his poems in their [[literary magazine]]. Save for xenophobic poems written during World War II, his poetry reflects the philosophy of [[humanism]] with an optimistic perspective on the world. He was a prolific author, publishing as many as 30-40 works per month. His poems tend toward minimalism and describe everyday events and scenes, without resort to excessive sentimentality.
His anthology, ''Jibun wa mita'' (“I Saw”, 1918), contains the poem ''Kuruma no oto'' (“Noise of the Carts”), which often appears in Japanese collections of Taisho period poetry. His longer work, ''Mukashi no ie'' (“House of Long Ago”, 1929), is [[autobiography|autobiographical]], describing his aristocratic background.<ref>''Japan: An Illustrated Encyclopedia'', Kodansha, vol. 2, p. 1345,</ref>

{{Infobox writer <!-- for more information see [[:Template:Infobox writer/doc]] --> | name = Senge Motomaro | image = Senge Motomaro.jpg | caption = Senge Motomaro | birth_date = {{birth date|1888|6|8|df=y}} | birth_place = [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]] | death_date = {{death date and age|1948|3|14|1888|6|8|df=y}} | death_place = | occupation = Writer | genre = poetry | movement = | notableworks = | influences = | influenced = }} {{family name hatnote|Senge|lang=Japanese}} {{nihongo|'''Senge Motomaro'''|千家 元麿||extra=8 June 1888 – 14 March 1948}} was a [[Japanese people|Japanese]] [[Japanese poetry|poet]] active during the [[Taishō period|Taishō]] and [[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] periods of [[Japan]].
==Biography== Motomaro Senge was born in [[Tokyo]] as the younger son of the [[Shinto]] high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]] in [[Shimane Prefecture]], who was also a member of the [[Japanese House of Peers|House of Peers]]. He was a member of the ''[[Shirakaba]]'' ("White Birch") literary circle, and published many of his poems in their [[literary magazine]]. Save for xenophobic poems written during World War II, his poetry reflects the philosophy of [[humanism]] with an optimistic perspective on the world. He was a prolific author, publishing as many as 30-40 works per month. His poems tend toward minimalism and describe everyday events and scenes, without resort to excessive sentimentality.
His anthology, ''Jibun wa mita'' (“I Saw”, 1918), contains the poem ''Kuruma no oto'' (“Noise of the Carts”), which often appears in Japanese collections of Taisho period poetry. His longer work, ''Mukashi no ie'' (“House of Long Ago”, 1929), is [[autobiography|autobiographical]], describing his aristocratic background.<ref>''Japan: An Illustrated Encyclopedia'', Kodansha, vol. 2, p. 1345,</ref>
==See also== *[[Japanese literature]] *[[List of Japanese authors]]
<references/>
==External links== *[http://www.aozora.gr.jp/index_pages/person617.html e-texts of works] at [[Aozora Bunko]]]]
{{Izumo-taishakyo}}

{{Infobox writer | name = Senge Motomaro | image = Senge Motomaro.jpg | caption = Senge Motomaro | birth_date = {{birth date|1888|6|8|df=y}} | birth_place = [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]] | death_date = {{death date and age|1948|3|14|1888|6|8|df=y}} | death_place = | occupation = Writer | genre = poetry | movement = | notableworks = | influences = | influenced = }} {{family name hatnote|Senge|lang=Japanese}} {{nihongo|'''Senge Motomaro'''|千家 元麿||extra=8 June 1888 – 14 March 1948}} was a [[Japanese people|Japanese]] [[Japanese poetry|poet]] active during the [[Taishō period|Taishō]] and [[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] periods of [[Japan]].
==Biography== Motomaro Senge was born in [[Tokyo]] as the younger son of the [[Shinto]] high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]] in [[Shimane Prefecture]], who was also a member of the [[Japanese House of Peers|House of Peers]]. He was a member of the ''[[Shirakaba]]'' ("White Birch") literary circle, and published many of his poems in their [[literary magazine]]. Save for xenophobic poems written during World War II, his poetry reflects the philosophy of [[humanism]] with an optimistic perspective on the world. He was a prolific author, publishing as many as 30-40 works per month. His poems tend toward minimalism and describe everyday events and scenes, without resort to excessive sentimentality.
His anthology, ''Jibun wa mita'' (“I Saw”, 1918), contains the poem ''Kuruma no oto'' (“Noise of the Carts”), which often appears in Japanese collections of Taisho period poetry. His longer work, ''Mukashi no ie'' (“House of Long Ago”, 1929), is [[autobiography|autobiographical]], describing his aristocratic background.<ref>''Japan: An Illustrated Encyclopedia'', Kodansha, vol. 2, p. 1345,</ref>
==See also== *[[Japanese literature]] *[[List of Japanese authors]]
<references/>
==External links== *[http://www.aozora.gr.jp/index_pages/person617.html e-texts of works] at [[Aozora Bunko]]]]

==Biography== Motomaro Senge was born in [[Tokyo]] as the younger son of the [[Shinto]] high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]] in [[Shimane Prefecture]], who was also a member of the [[Japanese House of Peers|House of Peers]]. He was a member of the ''[[Shirakaba]]'' ("White Birch") literary circle, and published many of his poems in their [[literary magazine]]. Save for xenophobic poems written during World War II, his poetry reflects the philosophy of [[humanism]] with an optimistic perspective on the world. He was a prolific author, publishing as many as 30-40 works per month. His poems tend toward minimalism and describe everyday events and scenes, without resort to excessive sentimentality.
His anthology, ''Jibun wa mita'' (“I Saw”, 1918), contains the poem ''Kuruma no oto'' (“Noise of the Carts”), which often appears in Japanese collections of Taisho period poetry. His longer work, ''Mukashi no ie'' (“House of Long Ago”, 1929), is [[:en:autobiography|autobiographical]], describing his aristocratic background.<ref>''Japan: An Illustrated Encyclopedia'', Kodansha, vol. 2, p. 1345,</ref>
==See also== *[[Japanese literature]] *[[List of Japanese authors]]
<references/>
==External links== *[http://www.aozora.gr.jp/index_pages/person617.html e-texts of works] at [[:en:Aozora Bunko|Aozora Bunko]]]]

<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[az:Senqe Motomaro]] [[de:Senge Motomaro]] [[es:Senge Motomaro]] [[fr:Motomaro Senge]] [[ja:千家元麿]] [[ru:Сэнгэ, Мотомаро]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Infobox writer | name = Senge Motomaro | image = Senge Motomaro.jpg | caption = Senge Motomaro | birth_date = {{birth date|1888|6|8|df=y}} | birth_place = [[Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | death_date = {{death date and age|1948|3|14|1888|6|8|df=y}} | death_place = | occupation = Writer | genre = poetry | movement = | notableworks = | influences = | influenced = }} {{family name hatnote|Senge|lang=Japanese}} {{nihongo|'''Senge Motomaro'''|千家 元麿||extra=8 June 1888 – 14 March 1948}} was a [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese]] [[:en:Japanese poetry|poet]] active during the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] and [[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] periods of [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
==Biography== Motomaro Senge was born in [[Tokyo]] as the younger son of the [[Shinto]] high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]] in [[Shimane Prefecture]], who was also a member of the [[:en:Japanese House of Peers|House of Peers]]. He was a member of the ''[[Shirakaba]]'' ("White Birch") literary circle, and published many of his poems in their [[:en:literary magazine|literary magazine]]. Save for xenophobic poems written during World War II, his poetry reflects the philosophy of [[:en:Humanism|Humanism]] with an optimistic perspective on the world. He was a prolific author, publishing as many as 30-40 works per month. His poems tend toward minimalism and describe everyday events and scenes, without resort to excessive sentimentality.
His anthology, ''Jibun wa mita'' (“I Saw”, 1918), contains the poem ''Kuruma no oto'' (“Noise of the Carts”), which often appears in Japanese collections of Taisho period poetry. His longer work, ''Mukashi no ie'' (“House of Long Ago”, 1929), is [[:en:autobiography|autobiographical]], describing his aristocratic background.<ref>''Japan: An Illustrated Encyclopedia'', Kodansha, vol. 2, p. 1345,</ref>
==See also== *[[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]] *[[:en:List of Japanese authors|List of Japanese authors]]
<references/>
==External links== *[http://www.aozora.gr.jp/index_pages/person617.html e-texts of works] at [[:en:Aozora Bunko|Aozora Bunko]]]]

Save for xenophobic poems written during World War II, his poetry reflects the philosophy of [[:en:Humanism|Humanism]] with an optimistic perspective on the world. He was a prolific author, publishing as many as 30-40 works per month. His poems tend toward minimalism and describe everyday events and scenes, without resort to excessive sentimentality.
His anthology, ''Jibun wa mita'' (“I Saw”, 1918), contains the poem ''Kuruma no oto'' (“Noise of the Carts”), which often appears in Japanese collections of Taisho period poetry. His longer work, ''Mukashi no ie'' (“House of Long Ago”, 1929), is [[:en:autobiography|autobiographical]], describing his aristocratic background.<ref>''Japan: An Illustrated Encyclopedia'', Kodansha, vol. 2, p. 1345,</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Senqe Motomaro]][[de:Senge Motomaro]][[en:Senge Motomaro]][[es:Senge Motomaro]][[fr:Motomaro Senge]][[ja:千家元麿]][[ru:Сэнгэ, Мотомаро]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Senqe Motomaro]][[de:Senge Motomaro]][[en:Senge Motomaro]][[es:Senge Motomaro]][[fr:Motomaro Senge]][[ja:千家元麿]][[ru:Сэнгэ, Мотомаро]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Senqe Motomaro]][[de:Senge Motomaro]][[en:Senge Motomaro]][[es:Senge Motomaro]][[fr:Motomaro Senge]][[ja:千家元麿]][[ru:Сэнгэ, Мотомаро]] {{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1888|6|8}}|death_date={{death date|1948|3|14}}|birth_place={{ill|Tokyo|WD=Q7473516}}|death_place={{ill|Tokyo|WD=Q1490}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|poet|WD=Q49757}}|native_name=千家元麿}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3325265]] Wikidata name: Senge Motomaro [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox writer | name = Senge Motomaro | image = Senge Motomaro.jpg | caption = Senge Motomaro | birth_date = {{birth date|1888|6|8|df=y}} | birth_place = [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | death_date = {{death date and age|1948|3|14|1888|6|8|df=y}} | death_place = | occupation = Writer | genre = poetry | movement = | notableworks = | influences = | influenced = }} {{family name hatnote|Senge|lang=Japanese}} {{nihongo|'''Senge Motomaro'''|千家 元麿||extra=8 June 1888 – 14 March 1948}} was a [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese]] [[:en:Japanese poetry|poet]] active during the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] and [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] periods of [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
==Biography== Motomaro Senge was born in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] as the younger son of the [[Shinto]] high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]] in [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], who was also a member of the [[:en:Japanese House of Peers|House of Peers]]. He was a member of the ''[[:en:Shirakaba|Shirakaba]]'' ("White Birch") literary circle, and published many of his poems in their [[:en:literary magazine|literary magazine]]. Save for xenophobic poems written during World War II, his poetry reflects the philosophy of [[:en:Humanism|Humanism]] with an optimistic perspective on the world. He was a prolific author, publishing as many as 30-40 works per month. His poems tend toward minimalism and describe everyday events and scenes, without resort to excessive sentimentality.
His anthology, ''Jibun wa mita'' (“I Saw”, 1918), contains the poem ''Kuruma no oto'' (“Noise of the Carts”), which often appears in Japanese collections of Taisho period poetry. His longer work, ''Mukashi no ie'' (“House of Long Ago”, 1929), is [[:en:autobiography|autobiographical]], describing his aristocratic background.<ref>''Japan: An Illustrated Encyclopedia'', Kodansha, vol. 2, p. 1345,</ref>
==See also== *[[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]] *[[:en:List of Japanese authors|List of Japanese authors]]

Save for xenophobic poems written during World War II, his poetry reflects the philosophy of {{ill|Humanism|en|Humanism|WD=Q46158|lt=Humanism}} with an optimistic perspective on the world. He was a prolific author, publishing as many as 30-40 works per month. His poems tend toward minimalism and describe everyday events and scenes, without resort to excessive sentimentality.
His anthology, ''Jibun wa mita'' (“I Saw”, 1918), contains the poem ''Kuruma no oto'' (“Noise of the Carts”), which often appears in Japanese collections of Taisho period poetry. His longer work, ''Mukashi no ie'' (“House of Long Ago”, 1929), is {{ill|autobiography|en|autobiography|WD=Q4184|lt=autobiographical}}, describing his aristocratic background.<ref>''Japan: An Illustrated Encyclopedia'', Kodansha, vol. 2, p. 1345,</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Senqe Motomaro]][[de:Senge Motomaro]][[en:Senge Motomaro]][[es:Senge Motomaro]][[fr:Motomaro Senge]][[ja:千家元麿]][[ru:Сэнгэ, Мотомаро]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Senqe Motomaro]][[de:Senge Motomaro]][[en:Senge Motomaro]][[es:Senge Motomaro]][[fr:Motomaro Senge]][[ja:千家元麿]][[ru:Сэнгэ, Мотомаро]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Senqe Motomaro]][[de:Senge Motomaro]][[en:Senge Motomaro]][[es:Senge Motomaro]][[fr:Motomaro Senge]][[ja:千家元麿]][[ru:Сэнгэ, Мотомаро]] {{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1888|6|8}}|death_date={{death date|1948|3|14}}|birth_place={{ill|Tokyo|WD=Q7473516|1=Tokyo Proper}}|death_place={{ill|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712|1=Empire of Japan}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17|1=Japan}}|occupation={{ill|poet|WD=Q49757|1=Poet}}|native_name=千家元麿}} {{Infobox writer | name = Senge Motomaro | image = Senge Motomaro.jpg | caption = Senge Motomaro | birth_date = {{birth date|1888|6|8|df=y}} | birth_place = {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | death_date = {{death date and age|1948|3|14|1888|6|8|df=y}} | death_place = | occupation = Writer | genre = poetry | movement = | notableworks = | influences = | influenced = }} {{family name hatnote|Senge|lang=Japanese}} {{nihongo|'''Senge Motomaro'''|千家 元麿||extra=8 June 1888 – 14 March 1948}} was a {{ill|Japanese people|en|Japanese people|WD=Q161652|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese people}} {{ill|Japanese poetry|en|Japanese poetry|WD=Q1683305|lt=poet|1=Japanese poetry}} active during the {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō|1=Taishō era}} and {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} periods of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.
==Biography== Motomaro Senge was born in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} as the younger son of the [[Shinto]] high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]] in {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}, who was also a member of the [[:en:Japanese House of Peers|House of Peers]]. He was a member of the ''[[:en:Shirakaba|Shirakaba]]'' ("White Birch") literary circle, and published many of his poems in their {{ill|literary magazine|en|literary magazine|WD=Q1368848|lt=literary magazine|1=Literary magazine}}. Save for xenophobic poems written during World War II, his poetry reflects the philosophy of {{ill|Humanism|en|Humanism|WD=Q46158|lt=Humanism|1=Humanism}} with an optimistic perspective on the world. He was a prolific author, publishing as many as 30-40 works per month. His poems tend toward minimalism and describe everyday events and scenes, without resort to excessive sentimentality.
His anthology, ''Jibun wa mita'' (“I Saw”, 1918), contains the poem ''Kuruma no oto'' (“Noise of the Carts”), which often appears in Japanese collections of Taisho period poetry. His longer work, ''Mukashi no ie'' (“House of Long Ago”, 1929), is {{ill|autobiography|en|autobiography|WD=Q4184|lt=autobiographical|1=Autobiography}}, describing his aristocratic background.<ref>''Japan: An Illustrated Encyclopedia'', Kodansha, vol. 2, p. 1345,</ref>
==See also== *{{ill|Japanese literature|en|Japanese literature|WD=Q231425|lt=Japanese literature|1=Japanese literature}} *[[:en:List of Japanese authors|List of Japanese authors]]
<references/>
==External links== *[http://www.aozora.gr.jp/index_pages/person617.html e-texts of works] at {{ill|Aozora Bunko|en|Aozora Bunko|WD=Q83466|lt=Aozora Bunko|1=Aozora Bunko}}]]
